--- SOURCE 1: Is there a problem with Online Archives and Bias? - LinkedIn ---
URL: https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/problem-online-archives-bias-brad-underhill

SUMMARY:
Selection is limited by the number of staff who can evaluate objects or the need to develop archival systems to automate appraisal.

--- SOURCE 2: [PDF] Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age ---
URL: https://www.burchardt.name/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Burchardt-Source-criticism-bias-and-representativeness-in-the-digital-age.pdf

SUMMARY:
This article critically examines digital newspaper archives, revealing error rates in optical character recognition (OCR) that compromise result reliability, and word frequency-based datasets that introduce biases due to issues in the shaping of the OCR corpus and later post-processing. Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 3 Digital historical newspaper archives demand not only traditional source criticism of the newspapers themselves but also scrutiny of every technical process between the printed pages and the researcher's screen. Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 12 If the search engine "improves" the results in this manner, researchers can opt for an alternative approach by searching in the raw OCR dataset, provided the newspaper archive offers such access.

FULL CONTENT:
Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age: A case study of digitized newspaper archives Jørgen Burchardt Museum Vestfyn Abstract Historians must critically scrutinize their sources, a task further complicated in the digital age by the need to evaluate the technical infrastructure of digital archives. This article critically examines digital newspaper archives, revealing error rates in optical character recognition (OCR) that compromise result reliability, and word frequency-based datasets that introduce biases due to issues in the shaping of the OCR corpus and later post-processing. Beyond technical issues, copyright restrictions hinder access to crucial newspapers, while incomplete archives pose representativeness challenges. Accessing datasets from different countries is cumbersome. Commercial archives are costly, and uneven publication rates necessitate corrections over time. The use of digital archives presents new exercises: the researcher needs to explain the reliability of the digital source, which often can only be achieved in interdisciplinary working groups. The digital archives must ensure transparency by detailing to researchers the technical manipulations performed on the original source. . Keywords 1 digital newspaper archive, OCR, source criticism, word frequency, bias, representativeness 1. Source criticism and digital archives Source criticism stands as an indispensable pillar of historical inquiry. As historians, we embark on the study of events distant in time, necessitating a deep reflection on the sources at our disposal. We must inquire into their origins, authenticity, and the inherent value they offer. In our quest to comprehend the past, we aspire to construct a comprehensive narrative, one that encompasses the entirety of historical reality. This demands an assessment of whether we possess a complete dataset, whether our sample is truly representative, and if crucial elements are regrettably absent. Overlooking these factors could lead to bias in our historical interpretations (Dobson and Ziemann 2020). In the sphere of digital history, source criticism is still maturing, lagging behind its counterparts in other social and cultural research fields. This is highlighted by Koch and Kinder-Kurlanda (2020) in their examination of big data. The systematic assessment of data quality in the digital realm is still emerging, with discussions primarily centered around provenance, authenticity, and integrity (Schmitz and Riebling 2022). The digitization of paper archives offers researchers two significant advantages. Firstly, it grants unprecedented access to historical sources, bypassing the slow, laborious process of library visits that were common two decades ago. Today, researchers can access sources anytime, anywhere. With the aid of search engines and other tools, locating and navigating documents has become remarkably DHNB2024 From Experimentation to Experience: Lessons Learned from the Intersections between Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage May 29 - 31, 2024, Reykjavik, Iceland. EMAIL: jorgen.burchardt@mail.dk www.burchardt.name ORCID: 0000-0001-9991-3499 ©� 2024 Copyright for this paper by its authors. Use permitted under Creative Commons License Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0). Digital Humanities in the Nordic and Baltic Countries Publications – ISSN: 2704-1441 Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 2 efficient through keyword searches. Additionally, each digital issue includes full-page images of the printed pages, often in easily accessible PDF files. Secondly, digitization unveils a new realm of possibilities for researchers through 'distant reading'—the analysis of long-term patterns and trends. Economic historians can examine large datasets generated in seconds, a feat impossible in the analogue world. Linguists can track shifts in complex semantic combinations, and political researchers can visually illustrate the networks of influential individuals. Bob Nicholson foresees a "cultural history 2.0," where researchers uncover new historical patterns through innovative methodologies. Nicholson's caution in his 11-year-old article about the risk of methodological discussions on digitization becoming outdated due to rapid technological advancements (Nicholson 2013, 62-63) remains valid. The Luxembourg Centre for Contemporary and Digital History (C2DH) shares this vision of a new era and has named its educational service for source criticism in the digital age "Ranke.2," after Leopold von Ranke (1795-1886), one of the fathers of German historical tradition (ranke2.uni.lu and Scagliola 2017). Ranke belonged to an old tradition that over the years developed professional hermeneutical historical scholarship based on understanding through source criticism, supplemented with interpretations of past ideas and meanings (Muller 2020). The debate over the extent to which historians need to comprehend software and digital techniques is ongoing. Media historian Huub Wijfjes explores the evolving role of historians in an era where the scope has broadened from close reading of a few limited texts to ‘distant reading’ of vast data sets (Wijfjes 2017, 4). He expresses concern that historians might also become computer scientists, or more diplomatically, ‘programming historians,’ as conceptualized in the textbook “Exploring Big Historical Data” (Graham et al. 2022). This work could be conducted in research groups that bring together diverse expertise, contrasting with traditional historical research typically undertaken by a single researcher. The average number of authors for an article in a contemporary digital humanities publication is around three, while traditional history is typically authored by a single researcher. New technology is reshaping the scientific landscape by introducing collaborative working methods (Rockenberger, Tiemann, and Gilbert 2023; Historisk Tidsskrift 2023 – see also Kemmann, 2021). Projects like the British Living with Machines exemplify this shift, featuring large interdisciplinary teams (Ahnert et al., 2023). In my view, historians who embrace these emerging avenues must delve into the technical algorithms underpinning them. This becomes paramount when utilizing the expanded digitalized capabilities, as discussed later in this article. Even traditional historians, embodying a 'History 1.0' ethos, should tread cautiously when using search engines to extract information from newspapers. While dates may prove reliable, seeking specific names or concepts can introduce biases. The traditional historian’s question remains: is this information accurate? Digital historians face an additional consideration: does our large dataset, created by complex search engines, contain bias? We are aware of the existence of OCR errors, so we know that our dataset is not 100% accurate. Nevertheless, we use it, but we must be mindful of potential bias. This topic is important and is currently under discussion. It was addressed at a development seminar in the impresso project, where the concept of ‘fairness’ was used to define the absence of bias (Ehrmann et al. 2023, 145). This discussion will continue in tandem with technological advancements. For example, improved datasets, thanks to the use of artificial intelligence and enhanced machine learning tools, pose challenges to cultural heritage institutions regarding bias (Neudecker 2022). It is worth mentioning that this article will not critique newspapers as a primary source. Although newspapers provide a wealth of information, their quality often suffers due to reliance on second-hand sources processed hastily by journalists facing tight deadlines. Notably, newspapers are also prone to misspelling names. While traditional researchers can mitigate inaccuracies by cross-referencing information from newspapers with other sources, historians employing large datasets or advanced search algorithms may inadvertently overlook these biases. Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 3 Digital historical newspaper archives demand not only traditional source criticism of the newspapers themselves but also scrutiny of every technical process between the printed pages and the researcher's screen. Evaluation of the technological environments used to create these new sources is imperative. Furthermore, attention to software handling, data contextualization, methodological operationalization, analysis, and interpretation is indispensable (Beals and Bell, 2020). In the words of Fridlund et al. (2020, 13), "it becomes evident that alongside the algorithms used, the selection, creation, cleaning, and filtering of the data heavily influence the results of the computer-assisted analysis." Figure 1: Search for milkmaid (‘mejerske’) in Danish newspapers. 2. Challenges with large datasets The complexities associated with utilizing large datasets, particularly those derived from newspapers, can be illustrated through an exploration of the evolution of a specific economic trade - the profession of milkmaids. The ensuing examples are intended to highlight the common challenges that similar projects and archives may encounter. For over a century, milkmaids played a pivotal role in the agrarian society of Denmark, demonstrating expertise in managing milk consumption and the production of cheese and butter. In the early 1800s, milkmaids were highly sought-after staff members on estates due to their ability to produce and sell top-quality dairy products. As the dairy industry became more industrialized, the role of milkmaids remained crucial, although men occasionally assumed these responsibilities. The progression of the milkmaid profession can be traced through an analysis of newspaper advertisements seeking such workers. These ads reflect the eagerness of large farms and independent dairies to connect with potential dairy employees. For example, a review of newspapers from the Danish newspaper archive, Mediestream, reveals mentions of milkmaids not only in advertisements but also within the main body of text (as shown in Figure 1). A closer examination indicates that during the first four decades, mentions were predominantly found in advertisements, with the first references within the body text appearing in articles celebrating birthdays. Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 4 Figure 2: Shift in standard of spelling from ‘meierske’ to ‘mejerske’. Figure 3: The red color shows the total number of results on ‘meierske’ and ‘mejerske’. 2.1. Evolution of Word Meanings Over Time Database searches are typically word-based, and it is widely recognized that a single concept can be described using various terms. For instance, the term 'boat' could refer to a 'canoe', 'sailboat', or 'motorboat', among many other possibilities. Consequently, a search for 'boat' alone might overlook results pertaining to various types of boats. To address this issue, some newspaper archives have implemented search features that allow for simultaneous searching of multiple terms. Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 5 Another challenge arises from shifts in the way words are spelled over time. In Denmark, for example, the modern spelling for 'milkmaid', 'mejerske', was formerly spelled as 'meierske'. Searches reveal an intriguing evolution in the standardization of this spelling over a span of 30 years, as depicted in Figure 2. In reality, a graph representing the mentions of this type of woman working at a dairy would resemble Figure 3. The blue graph presents a different trajectory compared to the initial search seen in Figure 1, indicating an earlier emergence of milkmaids. Understanding the historical evolution of words is crucial when conducting searches over extensive periods far removed from the present. For instance, the Danish word 'mejerske' once denoted not just 'milkmaid', but also a reaper. In the case of searches for 'mejerske', tests indicated that the 'reaper' meaning was rarely mentioned in newspapers. Otherwise, automated dataset generation from the search results would be impeded, necessitating the manual exclusion of reapers from the dataset—a laborious task best avoided. 2.2. Incomplete Search Results Due to Archive Limitations In an ideal scenario, a digital archive containing all newspapers from a country would provide a comprehensive overview of its societal landscape. However, as research by Hobbs (2013) and Milligan (2013) has revealed, scholarly publications increasingly rely on citations from readily available digitized newspapers, introducing biases into research. This bias is evident in a search for 'milkmaid', which yields disparate results between newspapers from a major city with few dairy cows and those from rural areas, as depicted in Figure 4. This bias is not exclusive to research on milkmaids. For instance, mainstream newspapers, often owned by affluent corporations and backed by advertisements from a traditionally conservative business sector, are typically more voluminous than their left-wing counterparts. This aligns with the critique of the Google Books Corpus (Pechenick, 2015). As argued by Beelen et al. (2022, 2), searches in digital archives necessitate a new form of source criticism that explicitly addresses representativeness and biases in digital heritage collections. Conversely, the analysis of digital archives can offer a critique of the original sources by identifying gaps in the coverage of society by paper-based newspapers (Beelen et al., 2023). Given this context, transparency about the inclusion and omission of newspapers in a database is essential for robust source criticism. However, the digitization of newspapers for archives is in many countries predominantly undertaken by private companies, which often lack interest in such documentation (Fyfe, 2016, 564). Table 1: Example of how free copyright is administered to newspapers by archive and country. Two main factors contribute to misrepresentation within archives. Firstly, many archives lack newspapers from certain years due to copyright laws. Despite international efforts to standardize copyright policies that grant content ownership until 70 years after the author's death, diverse policies persist for newspaper archives, and agreements with publishers are often necessary for broader access, as shown in Table 1. The rules are complex and often contain exceptions, such as in the United States, where publications older than 95 years or published without a copyright notice may be in the public domain (Clark, 2018). In contrast, Denmark's copyright limit is 100 years, while the Netherlands' is 140 years. Australia boasts the most liberal policy, allowing access to all newspapers published before 1955 as public domain material (Australian Copyright Council, 2012). Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 6 Directive 2012/28/EU in the European Union has facilitated the publication of orphan works, enabling libraries to include discontinued newspapers in their archives. For example, Delpher was able to overcome the Dutch copyright limitation of 140 years by including orphan newspapers in its collection. Other archives grant free access to researchers from restricted offices, as seen with Mediestream in Denmark and Nettbiblioteket in Norway. Alternatively, some archives approve access requests from researchers on a case-by-case basis. Figure 4: Search for ‘milkmaid’ in newspapers from the capital versus those from rural areas; the data is normalised. The second factor contributing to incomplete search results stems from the inherent limitations of database archives, which often lack significant portions of the newspapers they host. For example, a study on the 19th Century British Library Newspaper Archive uncovered instances of missing newspapers spanning many months or even entire years (Nicholson, 2012, 243–244). Digital archives of historical newspapers have only been in existence since a few years prior to the turn of the millennium, and the technology is still maturing. In 1993, the OCR program FineReader emerged as the first program capable of recognizing historical typefaces. By the late 1990s, digital readers had advanced enough to be deployed on a large scale, and the technology continued to improve through numerous ambitious projects (Bremer-Laamanen, 2006). For example, in 2004, the Analyzed Layout and Text Object (ALTO) standard was introduced to describe digital newspapers. Subsequent years saw the development of enhanced software programs, including the proprietary program Tesseract, which was originally developed in the 1980s, released as an open source in 2005, and sponsored by Google the following year. The first major digitization projects began in 1999, with PaperofRecord.com claiming to be the first online-accessible archive, primarily hosting newspapers from Canada and Mexico (Clark, 2014). That same year, NewspaperArchive was established, and ProQuest launched the Historical New York Times in 2000 (Hauswedell, 2020). In Britain, Thomson Gale Publishers, now simply Gale, introduced The Times Digital Archive in 2002 (Beals and Bell, 2020, 28). This foundational period was characterized by competition among various projects to be the sole providers of access to specific newspapers. Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 7 Historically, newspapers have often been digitized from their microfilm counterparts, a process that incurs significant expenses. The British Library initiated its digitization project in the United Kingdom in 2000, leveraging microfilm resources for entry into the digital realm (Fyfe, 2016, 564). Similarly, in the United States, companies like Heritage Microfilm and Readex served as the foundation for digital newspaper archives such as NewspaperArchive and America’s Historical Newspapers. Nordic countries also entered the digital archival landscape, with projects like TIDEN launching its database in 2001, prompting national archives to develop their own repositories (Bremer-Laamanen, 2006). Limited resources often necessitate prioritization in newspaper digitization efforts. For instance, in Denmark, only 130 out of 991 titles were identified as potential candidates for digitization (Bønding et al., 2014, 10). 2.3. Public vs. Private Archives Ownership plays a significant role in the accessibility of raw OCR data in digital newspaper archives, with three primary business models: library-based archives, commercial archives, and hybrid library-private cooperation archives. In the commercial model, a private company handles the digitization and distribution of the newspapers. This model aims to maximize profits through a revenue-sharing arrangement between the company and the newspaper publisher. The newspapers not only receive the archiving service for free, thereby eliminating upfront costs, but also earn royalties based on the usage of their publications. Users can search the archive for free and are charged only when they opt to view a full article or purchase a daily or weekly pass. This blend of free and paid content serves to enhance traffic and revenue generation. Since the early 2000s, four companies have dominated the digital newspaper archive business. NewsBank, the company with the most journals, publishes content for over 12,000 news source partners, with 5,000 of these publishing exclusively with them. The company also hosts websites for more than 1,000 newspaper publishers, enabling them to deliver their articles to their regular subscribers. They publish digital versions of both copyrighted and non-copyrighted newspapers. While most of these newspapers are from the United States, some are from Africa, Eastern Europe, Latin America, and Asia. NewsBank's newspapers are distributed by two specialized subsidiaries. GenealogyBank provides access to America’s Historical Newspapers, sold by subscription to individuals interested in genealogy, while Readex offers a subject-ordered catalogue that libraries can license (www.newsbank.com). ProQuest, the second major player, focuses its business on a select number of premium newspapers that it sells individually. Despite offering access to just over 60 newspapers, these publications include some of the most prominent ones, such as The Wall Street Journal, The New York Times, and The Washington Post, supplemented by unique newspapers, including African American and Jewish American historical newspapers. The collection includes newspapers from not only the United States but also other countries, including the Montreal Gazette, The Sydney Morning Herald, The Guardian, and the London Evening Standard. ProQuest has also acquired NewspaperArchive, which currently boasts nearly 16,000 titles from the United States and 46 other countries, with a particular focus on genealogy (www.proquest.com). Owned by Ancestry.com, Newspapers.com is the newest of the four dominant commercial archives and provides access to more than 25,000 papers (Newspapers.com). Lastly, Gale offers access to 18 million pages of newspapers. The most influential are premium newspapers such as the Financial Times, International Herald Tribune, Daily Mail, and The Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 8 Economist, supplemented with newspapers from other archives. The company’s suite of advanced search tools caters to both academic and educational users, providing scholars with efficient alternatives for accessing and analyzing content without requiring full database access. Its cooperation with The British Library is mentioned later (Gale.com and Nyhan et al., 2019). Although public archives are generally free to all, many newspapers have limited access as a result of copyright laws. Viewing all issues of some newspapers can be possible, however, if their copyrights have expired or if the archive has successfully come to an agreement with the copyright holders. One such public archive, Chronicling America, launched by the Library of Congress in 2007, remains a dwarf, with only 3,783 newspapers in its free public archive out of the more than 151,000 newspapers published in the United States (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov; Clark, 2014). Although many public archives allow searches in more recent newspapers, the results often do not provide users with full images of the newspaper pages. Libraries face significant costs when using commercial databases. The exact costs remain largely undisclosed as commercial firms do not publish their prices and libraries often have confidentiality obligations. For instance, when a blog published the exact amount charged by a company, the company demanded the information be removed (Mussell, 2011). Subscriptions to one of the most extensive archives could initially cost tens of thousands of British pounds, with subsequent annual access fees running into the thousands. Critics have expressed concern that digitization packages are primarily sold to academic institutions in North America due to financial constraints faced by British universities (Clark, 2014; Mussell, 2012). This suggests that the hybrid model is predominantly used for archives in the United States and the United Kingdom. For instance, the British Library, which established its archive in 2007, partnered with Gale Cengage to develop a searchable resource, partially funded by the latter. This collaboration extends to the online British Newspaper Archive managed by FindMyPast. However, access to the archive is limited to onsite users at the British Library or partnering libraries (Hauswedell, 2020). Some public archives have negotiated agreements with publishers to provide free access to all newspapers onsite at libraries, a practice also observed in Denmark and Norway. 2.4. Yearly Quantity Discrepancies and Misrepresentation Utilizing statistics for searches spanning numerous decades poses challenges due to variations in the quantity of published newspapers over time. In such cases, compensating for the uneven distribution of published words often necessitates a process known as "normalization" (Codd, 1971), an established technique in computer science. Normalization adjusts the weight of certain components to ensure a fair comparison, much like surveys where the weight of responses may be adjusted to balance demographics. In certain research contexts, normalization is indispensable. For example, when studying language evolution, it's common practice to compare equal amounts of words from each year, made possible by normalization, which enables fair comparisons. Consider the Danish newspaper Berlingske Tidende, originally known as Kiøbenhavnske Danske Post-Tidender, which published only eight pages in 1749 but expanded to over 120 pages on weekends in the 2010s. It is appropriate to multiply a single occurrence in 1749 by approximately 15 occurrences to equate it with the frequency 250 years later. A pertinent example is Google Books, the largest digitized literature database, which employs normalization and compensation techniques when presenting results in a time series chart. Here, frequency is computed by dividing the number of instances in a given year by the total number of words in the corpus of that year (Michel et al., 2011, 176–182). Similarly, searches in digital newspaper archives necessitate normalization, placing responsibility on database owners to furnish information regarding the number of words for each year. Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 9 Figure 5: Correction for amount of pages with exact and normalised results for “milkmaid”. However, most newspaper archives do not provide information on the number of newspapers published each year. Despite this, it is relatively straightforward to determine this total for normalization purposes. In an English language database, for example, a search for "the", one of the most common words, could be employed, although the validity of this methodology is open to debate (Younes, 2019). As depicted in Figure 5, normalization often yields a markedly different interpretation compared to simple searches, highlighting the importance of accounting for temporal variations in quantity. 2.5. Misrepresentation Arising from OCR Quality The quality of Optical Character Recognition (OCR) techniques is often gauged by counting errors. Despite calls for objective evaluation standards (Conway, 2011; Holley, 2009; Tanner et al., 2009), a straightforward system for measuring character accuracy remains undeveloped. A recent study employed a low-tech solution using random sampling to investigate the tendency of OCR quality in historical newspaper archives across 11 countries (Burchardt, 2023). Findings revealed low OCR quality for the oldest issues but improved accuracy for more recent decades. Figure 6 illustrates this trend, showing an increase in errors with the rise of broadsheets in the 1700s and the use of diverse fonts around 1900. Figure 7 presents a corrected search result for "milkmaid", highlighting a stark difference that would have been even more pronounced with more search results from years before 1845. The flaws identified align with those described in the guide, Optical Character Recognition (Nagy et al., 1999), attributing errors to damaged letters and challenges in word segmentation, particularly with hyphenated words across lines. However, poor OCR quality was observed in issues from one or several years across nearly all archives. While the poor condition of the original newspapers can account for most of these flaws, other factors such as the introduction of fracture font (the German typeface) and the use of italics also contribute. Some newspaper issues had error rates of 100%, indicating a lack of database proofreading. The average error rate across all archives and years is Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 10 estimated to be around 18%, a figure that underscores the importance of considering OCR quality when conducting scientific research using OCR-read text (Burchardt 2023, 42). Figure 6. OCR faults in words (percentage) (Burchardt, 2023). Figure 7. Search on “milkmaid” and corrected for OCR faults. High error rates pose significant challenges for sentence searches, with success rates falling below 50% for certain word combinations. For example, a search for the sentence "You speak an infinite deal of nothing" yielded a success rate of under 21% with a word error rate of 20%. These rates pose challenges for researchers aiming for success rates close to 100%, making claims of a text string's 'first-time' occurrence unfeasible. While the error rate for body text hovers around 18%, the rate for advertisements is notably higher. Although not extensively investigated, a cursory examination of advertisements from a single newspaper revealed a significantly elevated error rate (Burchardt, 2023, 43). Source criticism, bias, and representativeness in the digital age 11 Projects dealing with noisy data emphasize the importance of iterative data cleaning or refinement processes (Schöch, 2013). However, these approaches can be cost-prohibitive (Wijfjes, 2017), and many projects find themselves constrained by limited funds, exceeding the budget for desired iterative approaches (Nyhan et al., 2019, 32). A potentially more economical strategy to mitigate some errors involves rerunning the OCR process without generating new images of the newspapers (Schneider & Maurer, 2022). These error rates pose a significant challenge, particularly for searches in older newspapers, as they can lead to misleading impressions of the situation. One potential solution could be to correct the numbers. For example, as depicted in Figure 5, a correction of the search for 'milkmaid' in the Danish database was based on an average error rate derived from

SUMMARY:
Selection, Bias, & Silences - Researching with Archival & Special Collections Primary Source Materials - Library Guides at Ohio University 3.   Researching with Archival & Special Collections Primary Source Materials Researching with Archival & Special Collections Primary Source Materials *   Finding other archival & special collections materials Contact any of us (Bill Kimok for University Archives, Greta Suiter for Manuscript Archives, Miriam Intrator for Rare Books, Laura Smith for the Documentary Photography Archive) to learn more about these processes, if you're having trouble finding the materials you're searching for, to bring problematic issues related to our collections to our attention, or to let us know what else we can be doing to improve your experience with our materials.

**Subjects:**[Rare Books](https://libguides.library.ohio.edu/rare-books)

Selection, Bias, & Silences
---------------------------

One important thing to keep in mind when it comes to archives and special collections, to all collections really, is that they are not neutral spaces. It is impossible to preserve everything and so an individual or group has decided what will be kept, how it will be described, and how it will be made accessible and to whom. The flip side of that selection process is that over time, the vast majority of created materials have not been saved or preserved or are not accessible.

### What Is Missing?

Inevitably, historic and internalized, conscious and unconscious biases, including racism, classism, sexism, homophobia, and ignorance of marginalized genders, sexualities, or other communities, can all come into play during the selection process. In addition, historically, people with the most power and influence in society have considered the evidence of their lives to be important and worth organizing, describing, and collecting. Those without power and influence are usually both internally and externally excluded from that recognition and process. The result is reflected in phrases you may have heard, such as "archival gaps" and "archival silences." They remind us that so many voices, perspectives, experiences, and contributions have been lost, whether through purposeful exclusion or neglectful complacency.

Most traditional archives and special collections offer a skewed or inaccurate view of the past because of all those missing voices. Instead, they document a history and world told primarily from white, male perspectives. This problematic reality complicates research efforts. Some material no longer exists. Other material, however, can sometimes be found, it just takes some extra patience and creative thinking, searching, and questioning. Never hesitate to ask a librarian or archivist for help.

### What Are We Doing?

For our part, at the Mahn Center, we are beginning to take some reparative steps that we recognize as being both very small and long overdue. Our current actions include identifying, better describing, and bringing greater attention to materials already in our collections related especially to African American, LGBTQIA+, and women's lives, experiences, perspectives, and contributions, and to prioritize acquisition of similar materials. We are also beginning conversations to remove harmful or insensitive language and to add in inclusive and conscientious language when describing the materials in our collections.

Contact any of us ([Bill Kimok](https://www.ohio.edu/library/about/staff/kimok) for University Archives, [Greta Suiter](https://www.ohio.edu/library/about/staff/suiter) for Manuscript Archives, [Miriam Intrator](https://www.ohio.edu/library/about/staff/intrator) for Rare Books, [Laura Smith](https://www.ohio.edu/library/about/staff/smithle) for the Documentary Photography Archive) to learn more about these processes, if you're having trouble finding the materials you're searching for, to bring problematic issues related to our collections to our attention, or to let us know what else we can be doing to improve your experience with our materials.

### Learn More

Watch the video "[Identifying and Addressing Archival Silences in Your Research](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_whbBXD6t9c&t=529s)" to learn more (with grateful thanks to Michaela Ullmann).

There also increasingly exist institutions and organizations dedicated to broadening the available pool of materials by focusing on specific, historically excluded communities. See the Finding other archival & special collections materials tab in this guide for some possible starting places.

*   [**Next:** Critically approaching archives & special collections >>](https://libguides.library.ohio.edu/archives-speccollections/approaches)

## [Libraries News](/news) | [Special Collections](/news/category/special-collections)

# Ethics in Archives: Decisions in Digital Archiving

By: [Taylor de Klerk](/news/author/tkdekler)

*Blog post contributed by Taylor de Klerk and Jessica Serrao, Library Associates*

Digital archival work requires a lot of tough decisions. It may not always seem that way, but a lot has to happen behind the scenes before archival materials can be available to everyone on the Internet. Archivists have been trying to figure out the best way to incorporate digital assets into existing archival structures for quite some time. In our case, Special Collections has developed three pillars that make up our digital program: digitization, born digital processing, and web archiving. In this way, we organize our program by type of digital asset. Each of these pillars raise ethical questions as we work to fairly preserve, describe, and provide access to digital materials.

![Three pillars of SCRC's Digital Program](/sites/default/files/files/images/threepillars.JPG)

![Three pillars of SCRC's Digital Program](/sites/default/files/files/images/threepillars.JPG)

The first pillar is digitization. Most of Special Collections’ holdings are physical items. To improve access to these materials, we select and prioritize items to digitize, scan or photograph them, create descriptive metadata, conduct quality control, and make them available online on our [Rare and Unique Digital Collections](https://d.lib.ncsu.edu/collections) site.

Our second pillar is born digital processing. Unlike digitized materials, born digital materials were originally created in a digital format, such as a Microsoft Word document or a digital photo saved as a JPEG file. Our processing procedures ensure that those files are properly preserved--we use software to gather information about the files, document our actions, and create and manage archival packages that contain the original files and preservation documentation.

The third pillar, [web archiving](https://www.lib.ncsu.edu/web-archiving), is an endeavor in which Special Collections captures and saves instances of websites created by or affiliated with NC State University, as well as other donors. Each capture is a snapshot of what that website looked like on the date it was captured. We use [Archive-It](https://archive-it.org/) as our platform for this activity, which makes everything we capture available to view in the Internet Archive’s [Wayback Machine](http://archive.org/web/). You can access NCSU Libraries’ ArchiveIt homepage [here](https://archive-it.org/home/ncsu-libraries). For more details on the three pillars of our digital program, visit Taylor’s “Space Showcase: Digital Collections” post [here](https://www.lib.ncsu.edu/news/special-collections/space-showcase%3A-digital-collections).

Digital archives require so much specialized expertise that Special Collections has its own Digital Program Librarian: [Brian Dietz](https://www.lib.ncsu.edu/staff/bjdietz). He collaborates with the department’s curators, staff, and students to ensure that all three pillars of SCRC’s digital program are supported. One SCRC staff member who works closest with Dietz is Library Technician [Laura Abraham](https://www.lib.ncsu.edu/staff/lkabraha), who creates metadata for our digital collections and manages the collections’ overall quality.

![Brian Dietz, Digital Program Librarian](/sites/default/files/files/images/dietz.jpg)![Laura Abraham, SCRC Library Technician](/sites/default/files/files/images/abraham.jpg)

![Brian Dietz, Digital Program Librarian](/sites/default/files/files/images/dietz.jpg)
![Laura Abraham, SCRC Library Technician](/sites/default/files/files/images/abraham.jpg)

Digital materials require similar ethical decisions to those previously addressed in this series. However, when considering digital formats, these decisions take on new forms. For example, digitization costs a lot: it takes a long time, requires a variety of technologies, and needs a supportive storage system to ensure the security and longevity of the files. Because of those costs, Dietz works with other SCRC staff to prioritize materials for enhanced access. It’s a common misconception that everything donated to an archive is immediately digitized and available for public use.

To make decisions about those and other priorities, Dietz, along with colleagues in the SCRC, balances how researchers are using our collections with needs communicated by our curators. Dietz says, “it’s those two things together--what the curator is finding of value and wants to promote but also what researchers have expressed an interest in.” In this way, Dietz makes “data-informed instead of data-driven decisions” that consider what researchers are using as well as anticipating what they will want to use.

**Privacy**

Archivists must be vigilant about privacy when digitizing archival collections, processing born digital materials, or capturing Web content. We follow the same rules when digitizing as we do when processing, but there are additional facets to consider. For example, someone who wrote a letter in the 1960s could not have anticipated that letter being available online, so is it okay for us to put it there? Creators’ expectations of privacy, as well as the people who may be mentioned in the materials, are something worth thinking about when deciding whether or not to provide online access. If we do provide access to something that a creator or subject expresses discomfort with, Special Collections accepts takedown requests and reviews them on a case-by-case basis.

We also consider privacy when collecting social media content. This is different from written correspondence in that, as Dietz says, “some social media is intended to be public. It’s intended to be findable.” For born digital materials, we use applications like [BulkExtractor](https://www.forensicswiki.org/wiki/Bulk_extractor) to search for instances of personally identifiable information (PII), such as social security numbers or credit card information. Due to the nature of our collections, these are not common occurrences. If PII is found, we then make decisions as to how to manage it based on the nature of the information and privacy risks.

**Intellectual Property**

Similar to our respect for privacy, our policies also respect creators’ intellectual property rights. Careful consideration of copyright interests is an important part of digital archives. Pamphlets we find in folders, born digital photographs, websites, and other media may have a variety of copyright holders and restrictions for publication. To help us make decisions about rights, and to determine how to communicate those decisions to our users, we find [RightsStatements.org](http://rightsstatements.org/en/) to be a helpful tool. Navigating the legal jargon of copyright can be challenging, and there are often many grey areas that require us to make case-by-case decisions. According to Dietz, “assessing copyright is a risk assessment. We're far more likely to put no and low risk materials online. We need a strong justification to make things available online when there's little known about the rights situation.” As a public repository, our goal is ultimately to provide access, but not at the expense of the copyright holder. It’s a balancing act, just like many of these ethical decisions.

**Authenticity**

Another important ethical part of our digital program is ensuring that the digital records we create and preserve are authentic. Born digital materials in particular are easily modified or corrupted, and we use write protection software to mitigate that concern. Dietz says, “what gets into storage ought to be the same thing that left the work environment.” We verify [checksums](https://www.dpconline.org/handbook/technical-solutions-and-tools/fixity-and-checksums) at multiple points throughout the process to make sure that is the case.

We also have an obligation to ensure that our digitized materials authentically represent the original document or item that we digitized. To do this, we use high resolution scanners and cameras, make sure any coloration is accurate, and treat the files that digitization generates much like we do with born digital archival materials so that they can be indefinitely preserved.

For Web archiving, we limit our capturing to websites created by departments and units at NC State, as well as those we received permission from the site creator to capture. Websites beyond the scope of our servers can be created by anyone for any purpose, and therefore we only archive those that we trust.

**Preservation**

Digital archives aren’t immune to preservation’s ethical issues. Quite the opposite! Digital files can degrade over time (aka [bit rot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_degradation)), and digital storage is vulnerable to technical failures and obsolescence (old storage technology gets upgraded and files need to be moved to new storage). As a result, archivists are obligated to have a more active and iterative role in preservation over time than they would with paper materials. Papers, properly stored and preserved in a stable environment, can last a long time without intervention. Digital files will suffer if treated this way. Dietz works with other library groups and departments, including the [Information Technology (IT) department](https://www.lib.ncsu.edu/department/information-technology), [Digital Library Initiatives (DLI)](https://www.lib.ncsu.edu/dli/projects), and the Digitization and Digital Curation Working Group to develop preservation strategies and maximize efficient use of our storage environment. Files may be stored on fragile old media (think floppy disks), which are very vulnerable to degradation. When we process born digital materials, we move those files to a new storage environment to, as Dietz says, “liberate them from their legacy media jails.” Once in our stable storage environment, files need to be checked periodically for degradation or moved to new storage media as existing storage ages out. Dietz, IT, and DLI work to maintain, update, and check to ensure the Libraries’ environment is safe, stable, and reliable.

Check out this [cartoon](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pbBa6Oam7-w) for a fun overview of digital preservation. Additional information about bit level preservation and bit rot can be found in the Digital Preservation Coalition’s [Introduction to Digital Preservation](https://www.dpconline.org/docs/miscellaneous/events/2016-events/1662-bit-preservation-gettingstarted/file).

**Access**

Digital archivists are ethically bound to preserve and maintain digital materials so researchers can access them now and into the future. This means two different things when it comes to born digital versus digitized archival materials.

To ensure born digital materials are accessible, archivists must save the bits of each born digital file. This means battling technological obsolescence that can prevent access to the original files. There is a real risk that technology will fall out of favor and lose support from its creators and user communities. This can happen with file formats (AppleWorks’ .cwk files, .tga graphics files, etc.), digital storage media (floppy disks, compact discs, etc.), and even the software programs and hardware needed to read those files (WordStar, Windows 98, etc.). This isn’t unique to digital collections, and anyone who uses technology has experienced the ebb and flow of new technology in, old technology out. An amusing [article](https://www.cnet.com/news/top-ten-obsolete-ports/) from CNET highlights multiple ports that are now obsolete, which can become an archivist’s nightmare as external hard drives using these old ports can no longer connect to a computer to be read.

To ensure digitized archival materials are accessible, archivists must strive to provide unmediated web access. Making digital collections available online helps us to minimize geographic barriers to access and potentially to reach more diverse audiences. It is this positive exposure that could also be harmful to living subjects who are unknowingly represented in digital materials. As archivists provide increased access to digital materials, they must remember that some communities will still have limited access, such as those with little to no Internet availability. Other communities may be unaware that there are collections online which document their history. Additionally, there may be researchers who do not know online collections relevant to their research efforts exist. Online access is an improvement, but archivists must be aware that they may not be reaching all stakeholders (whether that is the documented communities, researchers, creators, etc.). Increased online access means more people are exposed to significant archival materials and archives need to inform, or even work with, stakeholders to provide fair and equitable access.

Archivists must constantly balance access with the time they spend on description. Should we take the time to describe materials more thoroughly, or quickly create minimal descriptions to get more materials available online? We can use tools like [Optical Character Recognition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Optical_character_recognition) to quickly make typed documents [full-text searchable](https://d.lib.ncsu.edu/collections/catalog/nubian-message-2001-04-05#?c=0&m=0&s=0&cv=0&z=-1494.4515%2C1544.1264%2C7329.1616%2C4621.9136), but even those materials require some additional description. Visual materials, such as photographs and AV recordings, rely more heavily on archivists’ descriptions, which directly affects how users find materials online.

**Description**

Describing digital materials has many of the same ethical concerns as describing physical materials. Various stakeholders have diverse values and identities that archivists need to represent fairly through descriptive practices. In addition to that responsibility, archivists need to be aware of how their own values and biases can creep into their descriptions. Archivists, subjects, and users may have conflicting claims to the language used to describe a record, but each claim should be considered empathetically in relation to each other and in relation to dominant power structures before any descriptive decisions are made. Laura Abraham, who creates descriptions for our digital collections, illustrates this point:

The Library of Congress has the authorized subject heading "Indians of North America," while many would initially think that this is anachronistic or even offensive. They may presume that "Native Americans" is what they should use as a better describer. However, the communities and individuals the terms represent are not unanimous in preferring "American Indian," "Native American," or something else. In addition, many want to be called by their tribal affiliation.

Abraham and others suggest the answer to this is getting those communities involved in descriptive practices. Abraham states, “We should ask the people the headings describe. What do the people of our campus and community prefer, how do they want to be represented?”  As collections documenting more sensitive subjects and vulnerable and marginalized communities are digitized and described, this will become an even more pertinent question.

Digital materials pose (and will no doubt continue to pose) challenging ethical situations for archivists to consider at every turn - from choosing materials to digitize or websites to capture, to describing the materials and providing online access. An open and empathetic approach can help archivists view their work from diverse angles and strive to make more ethically sound decisions. These decisions should be informed by those who are most affected by our work, whether that is the researcher, creator, or communities that have been historically unrepresented and underrepresented by archives.

This is the final post in our series about ethics in archives, introduced [here](http://www.lib.ncsu.edu/news/special-collections/ethics-in-archives). We hope this series has shed light on the tough ethical decisions that exist at every stage of archival work. Archivists do a lot more than just put papers in folders and folders in boxes. We have presented a variety of ethical issues that archivists face, and aimed to provide background information about what we do every day. Check out our previous posts on [privacy](http://www.lib.ncsu.edu/news/special-collections/ethics-in-archives:-how-special-collections-protects-your-privacy), [description](http://www.lib.ncsu.edu/news/special-collections/ethics-in-archives%3A-discoverable-description-and-accessible-arrangement-), [preservation](http://www.lib.ncsu.edu/news/special-collections/ethics-in-archives%3A-preservation-despite-the-odds), [curation](http://www.lib.ncsu.edu/news/special-collections/ethics-in-archives%3A-conscientious-collection-curation), and [social responsibility](https://www.lib.ncsu.edu/news/special-collections/ethics-in-archives%3A-diversity--inclusion--and-the-archival-record).

**Additional Resources**

Always Already Computational - Collections as Data. (2018). Santa Barbara statement on collections as data. Institute of Museum and Library Services National Forum at the University of California Santa Barbara. Retrieved from <https://collectionsasdata.github.io/statement/>.

Caswell, M. L. (2016). From human rights to feminist ethics: Radical empathy in archives. UCLA. Retrieved from <https://escholarship.org/uc/item/0mb9568h>.

Leach, E., Billey, A., & Hurst-Wahl, J. (2018). Can there be neutrality in cataloging? A conversation starter [Webinar]. National Information Standards Organization. Retrieved from <https://www.niso.org/events/2018/04/can-there-be-neutrality-cataloging-conversation-starter>.

Sims, Nancy. (2017). Rights, ethics, accuracy, and open licenses in online collections: What’s “ours” isn’t really ours. College & Research Libraries News 78(2). Retrieved from <http://crln.acrl.org/index.php/crlnews/article/view/9620/11028>.

--- SOURCE 5: Teaching With Digitized Archives, Some Challenges and Opportunities ---
URL: https://blogs.library.columbia.edu/rbml/2020/08/18/teaching-with-digitized-archives-some-challenges-and-opportunities/

SUMMARY:
Lost in Digitization. Important features of traditional archival research done in a reading room can be lost or rendered invisible to students

FULL CONTENT:
![Columbia University Libraries](https://toolkit.library.columbia.edu/v3/img/cul-text-logo.svg)
![Columbia University Libraries](https://toolkit.library.columbia.edu/v3/img/cul-text-logo.svg)

# Columbia University Libraries

# [News from Columbia's Rare Book & Manuscript Library](https://blogs.library.columbia.edu/rbml/ "News from Columbia's Rare Book & Manuscript Library")

## Teaching With Digitized Archives, Some Challenges and Opportunities

**Over the past several decades libraries around the world have spent enormous amounts of resources digitizing unique special collections. At latest count, the Digital Public Library of America contains more than 40 million documents. As instructors plan for remote learning, this wealth of digitized archival material presents an opportunity to teach students about archival research without having to access reading rooms, which remain closed or limited by the pandemic. But this wealth of material can also lead to misunderstandings of the nature of the historical record. This blog post discusses some of the challenges and opportunities of teaching with digitized archival collections.**

## Key Concepts to Consider

****Lost in Digitization.*****Important features of traditional archival research done in a reading room can be lost or rendered invisible to students when using digitized sources.*

**Context Collapse.**

Digitized archives can mask the radical imbalance of much of the historical record. The silences, erasures, and neglect of documents created by people of color, women, immigrants, and workers are hidden by the curated collation of archives into online datasets.

**Discovery.**

The opportunities for students and researchers in using digitized archives center around discovery. No longer limited to examining a single primary source, or a curated set of documents, students can use digitized archives to discover their own research projects. For the purposes of instruction, digitized collections used should be more or less complete. Students should be able to discern absence, as well as presence, of subject matter and materials.

**Resources for Archival Analysis.**

These documents provide further information about constructing classroom assignments for analyzing archives and archival sources:

## Columbia Libraries Collections for Instruction

#### These digitized history archives, accessible via the Columbia University Libraries, provide the type of completeness as a data set that will allow students to pursue a range of research learning tasks.

[Correspondence and Diaries from the Hubert H. Harrison Papers](https://dlc.library.columbia.edu/sites/hubert_h_harrison)  
*Hundreds of letters, photos, and documents created by one of Harlem’s most important early political activists.*

[Community Service Society Photographs](https://css.cul.columbia.edu/)  
*Almost 1400 photographs depicting urban poverty,  tenement housing, and other pressing social issue*

![Orchard Street](https://blogs.library.columbia.edu/rbml/files/2020/08/Orchard-Street-300x216.jpg)

*s in late-19th- and early-20th-century New York.*

[Herbert H. Lehman Special Correspondence](https://dlc.library.columbia.edu/lehman)  
*Correspondence with nearly 1,000 individuals from 1864 through 1982, including letters from every President of the U. S. from Franklin Roosevelt to Lyndon Johnson, as well as from notables such as Robert F. Kennedy, Fiorello LaGuardia, and Alfred E. Smith.*

[Carnegie Corporation of New York Digital Archive](https://dlc.library.columbia.edu/carnegie)  
*A portal into the Corporation’s philanthropy from the 1870s to the 21st century, from early twentieth century gifts in support of library construction to more recent interviews relating to the Corporation’s Russia Initiative.*

[New York Real Estate Brochure Collection [Avery]](https://dlc.library.columbia.edu/nyre)  
*Over 9,200 advertising brochures, floor plans, price lists, and related materials that document residential and commercial real estate development in the five boroughs of New York and outlying areas from the 1920s to the 1970s.*

![](https://blogs.library.columbia.edu/rbml/files/2020/08/125th-Street-from-L-Station-Looking-East-New-York-300x200.jpg)

[Photographs from the Frederick Fried Coney Island Collection](https://dlc.library.columbia.edu/sites/coney_island)  
*Photographs depicting the rich public life and popular culture of Coney Island from the late nineteenth to the mid-twentieth century, illustrating amusement rides; buildings and structures; parades, contests, and performances; the boardwalk; transportation; and people, both visitors to and employees of Coney Island’s various attractions, amusements, and spectacles.*

[Seymour B. Durst Old York Library](https://dlc.library.columbia.edu/durst) [Avery]  
*More than 40,000 objects including historic photographs, maps, pamphlets, postcards, books, and New York City memorabilia from the 18th century to the 1980s.*

## Other Collections for Instruction

#### These digitized history archives, accessible through other library websites, are also exemplary for student research learning.

[The George Grantham Bain Collection, Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/pictures/collection/ggbain/)   
*Has about 50,000 images, mostly depicting Progressive Era New York City.*

[W.E.B. Du Bois papers, UMass](https://credo.library.umass.edu/view/collection/mums312)  
*Contains nearly 100,000 original documents.*

[Independent Voices](https://voices.revealdigital.org/)  
*An open-access database, mostly featuring alternative newspapers from the 1960s and 1970s.*

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digitization often reflects biases based on staff evaluations and automated systems, leading to significant gaps in archived materials and underrepresentation of marginalized voices.
Rapid advancements in technology can render digital formats obsolete, risking the accessibility and preservation of archived materials if not continually updated and migrated.
Limited funding affects digitization projects, leading to prioritization of certain materials over others, which can compromise the comprehensiveness and longevity of digital archives.
